content.problemStatement:
You have prepared four problems. The difficulty levels of the problems are A1,A2,A3,A4A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4A1​,A2​,A3​,A4​ respectively. A problem set comprises at least two problems and no two problems in a problem set should have the same difficulty level. A problem can belong to at most one problem set. Find the maximum number of problem sets you can create using the four problems.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains four space-separated integers A1A_1A1​, A2A_2A2​, A3A_3A3​, A4A_4A4​,  denoting the difficulty level of four problems.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer - the maximum number of problem sets you can create using the four problems.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four space‑separated integers A1, A2, A3, A4 – the difficulty levels of the four problems.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the maximum number of problem sets that can be formed.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: all four difficulties are distinct, so we can form two sets, e.g., [1,4] and [3,2].\nTest case 2: frequencies are (1,3). Only one set can be formed using the distinct pair (4,5).\nTest case 3: all four difficulties are equal, no valid set can be created.

hidden.label: one pair and two distinct singles

solution.approach:
For each test case count how many times each difficulty appears. Let maxFreq be the largest frequency among the four numbers. The answer follows directly:
- If maxFreq == 4 (all four difficulties equal) → no set can be formed, answer 0.
- If maxFreq == 3 (three equal, one different) → only one set can be formed using the distinct pair, answer 1.
- Otherwise (maxFreq <= 2) we can always split the four problems into two sets of size two with distinct difficulties, answer 2.
This works because each set needs at least two problems with different difficulties and each problem can belong to at most one set.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per test case (only a few integer comparisons). Overall O(T).
